narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 52.6% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to make such decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, like your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 and LSAT score of 29, alongside your Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles of 3 and 2 respectively, your lower-middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. Compared to students who typically pass the bar, where successful applicants average much higher deciles around 6, an LSAT near 37, and a GPA of 3.24, your profile showed some gaps that weighed heavily in the model's view. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, as being in the lower part of your class there strongly signaled potential struggles on the bar, unlike the higher rankings common among those who succeed. Next in importance, your Year 1 decile also nudged the prediction toward failure, reflecting early academic challenges that often correlate with bar outcomes for students in similar situations. On a more positive note, your lower-middle family income actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing, since the model sees resilience in students from backgrounds like yours who still pursue full-time law studies. Overall, these academic deciles overshadowed the strengths in your profile, leading the model to flag a higher risk than for typical accepted students, who cluster in stronger performance brackets and income distributions leaning upper-middle or higher. While your full-time commitment aligns with the vast majority of bar passers, it wasn't enough to offset the key concerns. This isn't a judgment on your potential or drive—many students face similar hurdles and turn things around. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37, strengthening your recent grades to climb those deciles, or exploring programs with bar passage support tailored for full-time students from diverse backgrounds. Building a standout personal statement highlighting your resilience could also help in reapplying, and seeking feedback from admissions counselors on bridging these gaps might open new doors. With targeted improvements, you can shift your profile toward the successful averages and resubmit stronger.
